title: Quiz: Asymmetric Cryptography
description: Test your understanding of public-key cryptography, RSA, ECC, and quantum vulnerabilities

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between symmetric and asymmetric cryptography?

options.text: Asymmetric is always faster than symmetric
Asymmetric uses different keys for encryption and decryption
Symmetric uses public keys while asymmetric uses private keys
Asymmetric can only encrypt, not decrypt

questions.explanation: Asymmetric cryptography uses a key pair: a public key for encryption and a different (but mathematically related) private key for decryption. This solves the key distribution problem.

questions.question: What mathematical problem does RSA security depend on?

options.text: The discrete logarithm problem
The elliptic curve discrete logarithm problem
The integer factorization problem
The subset sum problem

questions.explanation: RSA security relies on the difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers (N = p × q). Given N, finding p and q is computationally infeasible for large primes.

questions.question: How does ECC P-256 compare to RSA-3072 in terms of security and key size?

options.text: ECC P-256 has weaker security and larger keys
ECC P-256 has equivalent security with much smaller keys
RSA-3072 has better security with smaller keys
They cannot be compared as they serve different purposes

questions.explanation: ECC P-256 provides approximately 128-bit security (similar to RSA-3072) but with 256-bit keys instead of 3072-bit keys—about 12x smaller. This makes ECC more efficient for the same security level.

questions.question: What does Shor's algorithm efficiently solve?

options.text: Symmetric key searching
Integer factorization and discrete logarithms
Hash function collisions
Lattice shortest vector problems

questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm can efficiently solve both integer factorization (breaking RSA) and discrete logarithm problems (breaking DH and ECC) in polynomial time on a quantum computer.

questions.question: In Diffie-Hellman key exchange, the shared secret is transmitted over the network.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. The shared secret (g^ab mod p) is NEVER transmitted. Each party sends their public value (g^a and g^b), and both independently compute the same shared secret. An eavesdropper cannot determine g^ab from seeing only g^a and g^b.

questions.question: What is the 'Harvest Now, Decrypt Later' attack?

options.text: Stealing encryption keys from hardware
Collecting encrypted data today to decrypt with future quantum computers
Breaking encryption by analyzing power consumption
Intercepting data during key exchange

questions.explanation: Adversaries can record encrypted communications today and store them. When quantum computers become powerful enough, they can use Shor's algorithm to decrypt all this stored data, even years later.

questions.question: Why is 'textbook RSA' (raw RSA without padding) insecure?

options.text: It's too slow for practical use
It's deterministic and vulnerable to various mathematical attacks
It doesn't support keys larger than 1024 bits
It requires the message to be exactly the key size

questions.explanation: Raw RSA is deterministic (same plaintext always gives same ciphertext) and vulnerable to attacks like chosen-plaintext attacks. Proper padding schemes (OAEP for encryption, PSS for signatures) add randomness and structure to prevent these attacks.

questions.question: Which of the following are broken by Shor's algorithm? (Select all that apply)

options.text: RSA
AES-256
ECDSA
Diffie-Hellman

questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm breaks RSA (factoring), ECDSA (ECDLP), and Diffie-Hellman (DLP). AES-256 is a symmetric algorithm unaffected by Shor's algorithm—it's only weakened by Grover's algorithm (to 128-bit security), which is still considered secure.
